text: AYRENS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of La Roquebron. Pop. 1,191.
AYRON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vienne, cant. of Vouillé. Pop. 772.

AYSGARTH, a parish in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 9 m. NW of Middleham. Area 79,980 acres. It includes the townships of High and Low Abbotside, Askrigg, Aysgarth, Bainbridge, Bishopdale, West Burton, Carperby-cum-Thoresby, Hawes, Newbiggin, Theralby, and Thornton-Rust. Pop. 5,725, of whom 269 were in the township of Aysgarth.
AYSTON, a parish in Rutlandshire, 1 m. N of Uppingham. Area 1,030 acres. Pop. 88.

AYTH, or AYT, a village on the coast of the Red sea, in N lat. 13° 55', and E long 41° 47'. It has a good road-stead, 9 or 10 fathoms in depth, and sheltered by a headland on the S. About 7 m. to the S is Ayth-Quoin, a lofty summit which forms an important landmark.

AYTON, a parish in the N. R. of Yorkshire, including the townships of Great and Little Ayton, and Nunthorpe. Area 5,740 acres. Pop. 1,216. Pop. of t. of Great A., which is 3 m. NE of Stokesley, 1,014; of Little A. 65.

AYTON, a parish and village in Berwickshire, stretching along the lower part of the Eye river and the sea-coast. Pop. of p. in 1831, 1,680; in 1841, 1,784, of whom about 600 were in the v. of Ayton, AZA which is 9 m. from Berwick, near the line of the North British railway.
AYTON EAST, a township in the p. of Seamer, N. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SW of Scarborough. Area 2,610 acres. Pop. 362.
AYTON WEST, a township in the p. of Hutton- Bushel, N. R. of Yorkshire, adjoining the above. Area 2,160 acres. Pop. 305.

AYTONA, or AITONA, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. of Lerida, on the r. bank of the Naguera, 3 m. above the confluence of that river with the Segre, and 11 m. SSW of the town of Lerida. Pop. 1,427.
AYTRE', a com. of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Rochelle. Pop. 1,320.
AYUMBO, a tribe who inhabit the northern district of the ESE of Damascus.
AYUQUITERAS, a fort of La Plata, in the Gran Chaco, on the l. bank of the Fogones, or Gabebiri river, about 120 m. above its confluence with the Paraguay.
AYUTLA. See AYOTLA.
AYWAILLE, a com. of Belgium, in the prov. and 12 m. SE of Liege, on the I. bank of the Ambleve. It possesses some iron-mines and works. Pop. 1,607.
AZA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 45 m. NNE of Segovia, on the r. bank of the Riaza, 5 m. above its confluence with the Douro.
AZAFI. See SAFFI.
AZAGRA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Navarre, in the partido of Estella, on the l. bank of the Ebro, 24 m. SSE of Estella, and 3 m. NNE of Calahorra. Pop. 1,356.
AZAK. See Azor.
AZAMBUJA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, in the comarca and 19 m. SSW of Santarem. Pop. 2,000.
AZAMBUXEIRA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, in the comarca and 13 m. W of Santarem.

AZAMOR, a maritime town of Marocco, in the prov. of Dukaila, near the mouth of the Morbeya—here about 150 ft. wide—120 m. NNW of the town of Marocco, in N lat. 33° 18′ 46", E long. 10° 24′ 15". It is surrounded by a wall and ditch, part of the original Portuguese fortifications. Its port is difficult of access from a bar dry at low water. A considerable quantity of wool from Mazagan is shipped here. A market of considerable extent is held weekly. Pop. variously stated at from 3,000 to only 600.

AZANS, or Assans, a tribe, now nearly extinct, of the Yenisee family, in the southern district of the Siberian gov. of Tomsk. They are governed by one of their own elders, and are remarkable for their peaceable demeanour and habits. They retain, it is said, the use of their ancient language.
AZAT-LE-RIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, cant. of Le Dorat. It has fine glass works. Pop. 662.
AZAY-BRULE', a commune of France, in the dep. of St. Maixent. Pop. 1,846.
AZAY-SUR-CHER, a commune of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Bléré. It produces good red wines. Pop. 1,355.
AZAY-LE-FERRON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Indre, cant. of Mézierès-en- Brenne, 16 m. N of Le Blanc. Pop. 2,001.

AZAY-LE-RIDEAU, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the arrond. of Chinon.—The cant. comprises 12 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 12,635.—The town, 13 m. NE of Chinon, and 15 m. SW of Tours, has manufactories of cards, linen, and bolting-cloth. Pop. 1,896.
AZAY-LE-THOUE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, and cant. of Secondigny. Pop. 1,140.
AZAZEH (EL), a town of Sennaar, on the l. bank of the Bahr-el-Azrek or Blue river, 13 m. below the confluence of the Bahad, and 70 m. NNW of Senaar.

AZCOITIA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Guipuscoa, partido of Azpeitia, on the l. bank of the Urola, 16 m. SW of St. Sebastian. It is situated in a plain surrounded by lofty mountains, amongst which the Izarriz contains quarries of marble and jasper. Sulphureous and ferruginous thermal springs exist in the vicinity. Pop. 3,490.

AZE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Loiret-Cher, and cant. of Vendome. Pop. 1,710.—Also a com. in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Chateau-Gontier. Pop. 1,206.—Also a com. in the dep. of Saôneet-Loire, cant. of Lugny. Pop. 1,313.
AZEBEDO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 40 m. NE of Leon, on the l. bank of the Esla, and near the southern base of the Sierra-Cabadanaga.
AZEITAO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, 13 m. SE of Lisbon, and 6 m. WNW of Setubal. It has manufactures of printed calicoes, and some trade in wood and dye-stuffs. Pop. 2,800.
AZENDORF, or ATZENDORF, a town in the duchy and 15 m. SSW of Magdeburg. Pop. 800.
AZERABLES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Creuse, cant. of La Souterraine. Pop. 1,964.
AZERAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Thenon. Pop. 1,364.
AZERAILLES, a commune of France. in the dep. of Meurthe, cant. of Baccarat. It has extensive limekilns, and brick and tile-works. Pop. 816.

AZERAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Loire, cant. and 2 m SE of Auzon, on the r. bank of the Allier. Pop. 626. AZERDBIJAN [Monteith], AZERBIJAN [Kinneir], the extreme NW prov. of the Persian empire, situated between 37° and 39° N lat.; and 44° and 49° E long. The river Araxes separates it on the N from Russian Armenia; on the E it has the Caspian sea; and on the SE the Kizil-Ozein river, which separates it from Irak-Ajemi; on the S it borders on Kurdistan; and on the W, on Turkish Armenia. The general features of the surface consist of "a regular succession of undulating eminences, partially cultivated, and opening into plains." [Kinneir.] Near the centre of the prov., between Tabriz and Maraja, the lofty mountains of Sahend tower in an isolated mass to the alt. of 9,000 ft. above sea-level. Their base covers a space of some hundred m. in circuit, in which the general formation is conglomerate resting upon a base of granite. The summits are porphyritic. [Fraser.] In the vicinity of Ardebil, towards the E frontier, Mount Sevellan approaches an alt. of 13,000 ft., and exhibits volcanic rocks and beds of lava. [Monteith.] The Talish range, to the E of Ardebil, runs from N to S in a course nearly parallel to the shores of the Caspian, and attains an alt. of 7,000 ft. [Fraser.] The Kaflankuh, a branch of the mountains of Kurdistan, constitutes a part of the S frontier of this prov. The principal rivers are the Araxes, the Kizil- Ozein, and the Jugatty. The Yezdican and the Harasa are tributaries of the Araxes. The Agi and the Shar flow into the great salt lake of Urmiah or Urumea, which constitutes one of the most remarkable features of A. Its water is so salt that no fish can exist in it, and so buoyant that it is difficult to retain one's footing in a depth of 3 ft. [Monteith.] The climate of A. is healthy. Intense cold is often experienced on the high lands; and some of the mountains and mountain-ranges are never quite clear of snow. Fraser, entering A. from Ghilan, found \ AZI apricots, plums, and cherries, which were perfectly ripe in the latter prov., little more than setting in the neighbourhood of Ghilivan. The finer species of fruit does not ripen well in the environs of Ardebil, being frequently blighted by NE winds blowing from the Caspian over mountains whose summits are covered with snow. A. is reckoned one of the most productive prov. of Persia; and the villages, embosomed for the most part in orchards and gardens, are more numerous, and present a more pleasing appearance than even those of Irak. [Kinneir.] The cultivation of the land is chiefly promoted by irrigation. The plough is drawn by oxen; and the best soil yields a return of from 50 to 60 fold. Iron, copper, and lead exist in the Kaflankuh, and probably in other districts of this prov.; but its mineral treasures have never been explored.—The principal towns are Tabriz, the cap., in N lat. 38° 3′ 59", E long. 46° 8' 30"; Ardebil, in N lat. 38° 10' 20"; Maragha, in N lat. 37° 9′ 50"; and Urmiah on the SW side of the lake to which it gives name.—Kinneir's Geographical Memoir. - Colonel Monteith in Geog. Jour., vol. III.—Fraser's Travels on the Southern banks of the Caspian.
AZERE (PINHEIRO DE), a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, and comarco of Arganil, 25 m. SSW of Viseu.
AZEREIX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrénées, cant. of Ossun. Pop. 1,004.
AZERLEY, a township in the parish of Kirkby- Malzeard, in the W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 3,140 acres. Pop. 836.
AZEUCHAL, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Estremadura, on the r. bank of the Guadaljira, 25 m. SE of Badajos.

AZEVEDO, a river of Brazil, which takes its rise in the prov. of Matto-Grosso, in the mountainous district of the Cabahybas territory, and running in a NW direction, falls, after a course of 240 m., into the Tapajos, in S. lat. 8° 30'.
AZIARCOLLAR, a town of Spain in Andalusia, in the prov. and 15 m. NW of Seville.
AZIBO, a river of Portugal, in the prov. of Trasos-Montes, which takes its rise in the Sierra Ribardao, and, running to the SE, unites with the Sabor at Castro-Vicente.
AZIEVO, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Tambov, 11 m. SE of Elatom.
AZILLE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aude, cant. of Peyriac, near the N bank of the canal of Languedoc, 14 m. ENE of Carcassonne. It has extensive tanneries. Pop. 1,498.
AZIMABAD, a small town of Hindostan, 7 m. NW of Kurnal.

AZIMGHUR, or AZIMGUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Allahabad, on the l. bank of the Tonse, an inosculation of the Kalee or Gogra, and the Ganges, 42 m. NE of Juanpur. The cotton manufactories of this town and of its vicinity are considerable, and constitute its principal commerce. A. was ceded to the British by the nabob of Aude in 1801.

AZIMNAGUR, an extensive district of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bejapur, to the S of the Krishnah. It is watered by the Gutpurba and Malpurba; but is to a great extent uncultivated. Its principal towns are Gokak, Belgaum, and Shahpur.

AZIM-SAHIB-KA-SERIA, a serai in the province of Malwa, one of the largest and handsomest in Hindostan, situated in a fertile valley of the Vindiya mountains, between the passes of the Jaumun and Koleetee ghauts, 8 m. SW of Mandu. Its outer walls, strongly built of granite and brick, are fortified with bastions and loop-holes. The galleries surrounding and opening into the interior quadrangle are constructed of red marble. This serai, and the ruins of Mandu, were formerly strong-holds of the | the NE arm as to permit of communication, by Bheel robbers, by whom this locality was infested.

AZINCOURT, formerly Agincourt, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, and canton of Le Parcq, 12 m. NW of St. Pol, and 36 m. SSE of Calais. It is famous as the scene of the decisive victory gained by the English over the French, on the 25th October 1415.
AZINHAGA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, 8 m. NE of Santarem, on the road from that town to Punhete.
AZINHAL, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Algarve, on the r. bank of the Guadiana, 5 m. N of Castro Marim.
AZIO, a village of Greece, in the nomarchy of Acarnania, on the SE side of the gulf of Prevesa. It is supposed by some to mark the site of the ancient Actium.
AZIZPUR, a village of Sinde, 18 m. SW of Shikarpur, on the E bank of the Indus.

AZMERIAGUNGE, a town of Hindostan, in the district of Silhet, in Bengal, on the l. bank of the Surmah, 33 m. above its confluence with the Ganges, and 75 m. NW of Dacca. It possesses an active inland trade, and an extensive boat-building establishment. The influence of the tide is felt at this place in the dry seasons, there being a rise and fall of about 1 foot in springs.
AZMOOS, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of St. Gale, near the l. bank of the Rhine, 3 m. NNE of Sargans.
AZNALCAZAR, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Andalusia, 7 m. SW of Seville, on the l. bank of the St. Lucar.

AZOF (SEA OF), an inland sea or lake, the More Asowskoe of the Russians, and Palus Mæotis of the ancients, which communicates with the Black sea, on the S, by the strait of Yenikalé or Taman; lying between 45° 20' and 47° 18' N lat., and 34° 50' and 39° 5' E long. Its length, from the strait of Kaffa or Yenikalé,—the ancient Bosphorus Cimmerius—to the mouth of the Don, is 168 m., of 60 to a degree; and from the Tonika to the centre of the point of Kamyshevata 123 m. On the parallel of 46° it measures 116 m.; on the meridian of 37°, 84 m. It is surrounded on all sides by the Russian territories: having on the NE the government of the Don-Cossacks of Ekaterinoslav, and part of the gov. of Taurida; on the E and S, the gov. of Taurida; on the SE and E, the gov. of the Tchernomovskoi-Cossacks; and on the W, the Sivash or Putrid sea, which lies between it and the peninsula of Arabat. On the NE it shoots out into a long horn or bay about 70 or 80 m. in length, and from 20 to 40 m. in breadth. The area of the whole is about 14,000 sq. m. Its northern shore is elevated only from 115 to 130 ft. above the water-level. The sea-face here is steep, and shows marly calcareous strata, yellowish or reddish coloured: a few small eminences appear scattered over it at a considerable distance from each other. Its eastern shore, inhabited by the Tchernomovskoi-Cossacks, is very low, often sandy, or interrupted by swamps and mud-banks. A narrow sandy tongue of land, 52 m. in length, and running NNW and SSE, called Tonika or Arabat, which separates the Sivash from it, forms its W side. The strait of Ghenitchi or Yenitchi, which forms the communication between the sea of Azof and the Sivash, is a deep channel about 60 fathoms in breadth at the N extremity of the Tonika. The low mountains of the Crimea, and the isle of Taman, border the S coast. These mountains in some places present volcanic appearances. The strait of Yenikale is lined on both sides by low sandy hills. During winter the sea of A. is frozen over, except towards the centre, for a month; and the ice is so strong in I. AZO land carriage, between Taganrog and the city of Azof, 30. m. distant from each other. It is seldom sufficiently clear of ice for navigation before April. " From the mouth of the Don to the heights of Taganrog," says Pallas, " the sea is every winter so completely frozen that travellers cross in carriages upon the ice with the greatest safety. The whole shore is also to a very considerable extent covered with ice; it, however, often breaks in consequence of sea-storms, and the fishermen are exposed to great danger from this cause. The sea generally freezes in December, and remains in that state till March; but shoals of ice in the Bosphorus delay navigation till a much later period in the spring." Two rivers of large volume debouch into this sea by a great number of arms: the one is the Don, which enters it on the NE, at the upper extremity of the bay of Taganrog; the other is the Kuban, one branch of which called the Tchernoiprotok, or Black-water, falls into this sea to the E of Point Atchuiev. The Kalmius, which falls into this sea to the E of Mariopol, admits small barks. The Berda, which flows into it on the SE, has its mouth near Cape Berdiansk .- It has been a received opinion since the days of Polybius, that this sea is constantly diminishing in depth, and must in process of time be filled up; but recent observations show this to be erroneous. In the vicinity of Taganrog, near the mouth of the Don, the water is indeed very shallow, varying from 3 to 10 ft., according to the direction of the wind; but according to the observations of Gautier-who thoroughly examined this sea-all over the rest of its expanse, the soundings vary from 40 French feet in the centre, to an average of 17 or 18 ft. close in shore. The greatest depth is between Yenikale and Bielosarai, where it is 46 ft. In the Cimmerian Bosphorus, or strait of Yenikale, indeed, the water is shallow, as it was in the days of Polybius; and it must always be so from the crookedness and extreme intricacy of the passage, which at once prevents the fair rush of the stream from the northward, and favours the accumulation of deposit. Here the soundings in the shallowest part are 13 French ft.; but in that part of the passage which opens into the Euxine, the soundings deepen from 4 to 20 fath., and more when the open sea is reached. On the eastern side of the channel, though the soundings are on mud, yet they change in the course of 5 m. to sand and mud, and afterwards to shells; whilst in the middle of the passage they are continually upon shells, or sand and shells; a clear proof that no accumulation is going on in the strait, but that even there the rush of water from the less sea to the greater is sufficient to keep the channel clear, and to obviate the inconvenience apprehended by Polybius. The water of the sea of A. is turbid and brackish, especially after SW winds have impelled a large influx of water from the Black sea; and these winds are the most constant in spring and summer. The Don, as well as several other streams, sweeten it on the NE; and it becomes drinkable at a distance of 20 m. from Taganrog. No rapid current is observed in this sea. It abounds in fish, and its fisheries are important and valuable. According to official reports there were in 1840, 323 vessels manned by 1,517 hands navigating this sea. These vessels are lighters, and carry goods from Taganrog, Mariopol, Rastof, and Bordiansk to the vessels moored in the straits of Yenikalé. The practical knowledge of this sea obtained by the Venetians and Genoese was long completely extinguished; but since it fell under the ascendency of the Russian power, several charts of it have been published, as that of Admiral Kreutz in 1699, after the capture of Azof in 1696, and the observations of Lieut. Budischiff in 1808; and an excellent and complete chart | was published at Paris in 1822, from the observations of Gautier, Benoist and others.—Clarke's Travels.— H. de Hell's Travels.—Hagemeister's Commerce of the Black sea.—Pallas's Travels. AZOF [TURK. Azak], a city of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Ekaterinoslav, on the left bank of the southernmost branch of the Don, nearly 20 m. E of the entrance of that river into the above sea, and 840 geographical m. SE of St. Petersburg. After undergoing many revolutions, all fatal to its prosperity, it was taken from the Turks in 1696 by Peter the Great, who restored it by the treaty of the Pruth, in 1711. Again taken by Marshal Munich, in 1736, it was again restored to the Turks in 1739, on condition of their demolishing the fortifications. In 1769 it was retaken without resistance, rebuilt, and repeopled by the Russians; and in 1774 was finally ceded to them by the peace of Rainardji. The whole country round A. is full of swamps; the town stands on the only spot capable of admitting a solid edifice, while to the E, SE, and S, the interior of the country presents a parched and barren desert. [Clarke.] The place has fallen almost completely to decay, having scarcely 50 houses. The works are in a ruinous state; and the garrison consists of a few invalids.

AZOGUES, a town of Ecuador, in the prov. and 15 m. NNE of Cuenca, in the fertile valley of the Gunguilla, a head-stream of the Pante and Santiago. It has it name from the mines of quicksilver which formerly existed in the locality. Silver, in small quantities, is still found in the vicinity.
AZOR, a village of Syria, 8 m. E of Ascalon, and 32 m. WSW of Jerusalem.

AZORES, or Western Isles, a group of islands in the Atlantic ocean, between 37° and 40° N lat., and 25° and 31° W long. They have generally been considered as belonging to Africa, though they are almost equidistant from Europe, Africa, and America. Their name is said to be derived from the vast number of goshawks [Falco milvus], called by the natives acor, which were seen upon them when first discovered. They are called the Western islands from their situation with respect to Portugal; and by the Portuguese they are sometimes called the TERCEIRAS, from the island of that name in the group. They were discovered in 1439, and first peopled by Europeans; and their pop. amounted, in 1841, to 214,300. They are a possession of Portugal; but the pop. has a mixture of Flemish and of Spanish blood, which has improved the breed. The peasantry are "upright, muscular, and well-knit;" and the islands lie so out of the way of the world, that the inhabitants, especially of the less-frequented isles, retain all the ignorance, simplicity, bonhommie, and other traits not quite so respectable, which distinguish the ruder classes of Southern Europe. — The group consists of nine islands arranged in an irregular line, and at unequal distances, in a WNW 1 W direction: St. Michael's and St. Mary's, closely adjoining each other; Terceira, Fayal, Pico, Graciosa, and St. George's, nearly a group by themselves; and Corvo and Flores, considerably to the westward, that is, more towards America. Their aggregate area is about 700 sq. m. They are of volcanic origin, and have often suffered severely from earthquakes and volcanic eruptions; and possess the characteristics of such islands,-floods of lava, extinct craters, precipitous mountains frequently forming one central peak, a friable soil, and a dangerous coast. There is no active volcano on the islands at present; but the rising of new islands from the bottom of the sea in their vicinity-a phenomenon which has frequently occurred-indicates the presence of active subterranean fires. In 1811 a submarine explosion gave formation AZO to an island which rose 300 ft. above the sea, and had a crater 500 ft. in diam.; but the whole, composed probably of beds of secondary tufa, was speedily washed down by the sea. The aspect of all the islands is similar in general characteristics, presenting an elevated and undulating outline, with little or no tableland, and rising into peaks, of which the lowest, that of St. Mary's, is 1,889 ft., and the highest, that of Pico, 7,613 ft. above sea-level. Their coasts are, with few exceptions, high and precipitous. Though densely wooded when first discovered, they are so no longer: a few young plantations of small extent, and orange-gardens, with an occasional expanse of uncultivated ground covered with heath and shrubs, cover much of the surface. The vegetation is a mixture of that of Europe and the tropics. Wheat and bananas, figs and cabbages, oranges and potatoes, are equally abundant and excellent in their kind. [Ward.] The animals and birds are those of Britain; the fishes, of a mixed British and West Indian character; the insects and plants, partly British, partly Spanish and Portuguese. No poisonous animal or reptile is found in these islands. Climate.] Situated in about the same latitude as the extreme south of Europe, the climate is warm throughout the winter; and it is constantly tempered by sea-breezes, blowing over many thousand miles of ocean. It also varies less than that of any other place; but the extremes of heat and cold are increased by the degree of humidity present in the atmosphere. Observations made by Dr. Buller during his stay at St. Michael's, furnish some interesting facts upon this point, both positive and comparative. "The mean in-door temperature of a room in Villa Franca, without fire, curtains, or carpet, during the winter-months, is 60°, with a mean range of 3°; and the out-of-door temperature during the day for the same period presents a mean of 60° with a range of 7°. The mean difference between the climate indoors and out-of-doors is so trifling as scarcely to deserve notice; being no more than a small decimal fraction of a degree. There is, however, a difference of 4° between the range of the therm. in the house and out-of-doors. Comparing the climate of Villa Franca during the winter-months with that of the south of England in the summer-time, it appears from Mr. Giddy's meteorological journal, that the average mean temperature of the Land's End in the month of August, during a period of twelve years, was precisely the same as that of the town of Villa Franca in St. Michael's, during the months of December, January, February, March, and April; over which the foregoing observations extend, namely 60° 9'. The mean temperature of the winter-months in St. Michael's, according to these observations, is 2° colder than Madeira, 5° warmer than Lisbon, 13° warmer than Nice, and 12° warmer than Rome and Naples." The highest known extreme of July, near the level of the sea, is 86°. Productions.] The soil of the A. on the hills and valleys adjacent to the sea is highly fertile; but agriculture is rude, and shackled by a system of entail, under which the lands are held. The productions are grain, wine, oranges, lemons, bananas, the sugarcane, the coffee-plant, and tobacco. The valuable Lichen roccella is extensively produced. About 130,000 boxes of oranges, and 2,000 pipes of wine and brandy, are annually exported to England. To Brazil the average exports are 5,000 pipes of wine, and 12,000 vards of coarse linen; and the markets of Lisbon, Oporto, and Madeira, are supplied from these islands with wheat, barley, and pulse of all sorts.-They are said to yield a clear revenue of 330,000 crowns to Portugal. St. Mary's, the most southern of the Azores, is fer tile in wheat, Indian corn, and other kinds of grain. The number of inhabitants in 1840 was 4,666.—St. Michael's, situated in 37° 47' N lat., and 25° 42' W long., is more populous than any other of the group, being 80,809 in 1840. It has two good harbours, Ponta del Gada, which is the capital, and Villa Franca.—Terceira, in 38° 45' N lat., and 27° 6' W long., though not the largest, nor the most populous, is generally considered as the chief of the Azores. Its circumference is about 45 m.; and its pop. is said to be about 20,000. The governor of the Azores usually resides in its cap, Angra.—Fayal, in 38° 32' N lat., and 28° 41' W long., is a considerable island; and the most frequented of the whole group, its harbour being not only the best, but also lying directly in the tract of vessels crossing the Atlantic. Its pop. amounts to 18,000.—For full details respecting these, and the other islands of the group, the reader is referred to our special articles on each. Authorities.] Hebbe, Descript. de l'isle Fayal et des autres Açores. Traduite par J. B. B. Eyries. - Bold's Description of the Azores, 1835.—Consul Hunt in Geog. Journ., vol. xv.
AZOUDANGE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, cant. of Richecourt-le-Chateau. In the vicinity are quarries of gypsum. Pop. 498.

AZPEITIA, a partido, and a walled town of Spain, in the prov. of Guipuscoa, on the l. bank of the Uróla. The town, 8 m. WNW of Tolosa, and 7 m. E of Elgoybar, is situated in a plain surrounded by lofty mountains, which afford excellent pasturage. Its principal building is the church of St. Ignace-de- Loloya, which stands on the opposite side of the river. It has several iron-works and manufactories of nails, and some of copper and iron hardware, Pop. 3,872. In the neighbourhood are the hot springs of Guezalaya.
AZREK (BAHR-EL). See ABAI.
AZTECS. See MEXICO.

AZUA-DE-COMPOSTEL, a town on the S coast of Hayti, 2 m. N of the bay of Occa, 55 m. W of St. Domingo, and 105 ESE of Port-au-Prince. It was founded in 1504 by Diego Velasquez, and was originally called Compostella; but has since assumed the appellative Azua. In 1751 it was destroyed by an earthquake, but before the close of the century consisted of 300 houses with a pop. of 3,000. The sugar-cane is cultivated in the surrounding district.
AZUAGA, or AZUEGA, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. of Badajos, and partido of Llerena, 20 m. ESE of Llerena. Pop. 5,344.
AZUCAR, or AZACAR, a town of Chili, in the prov. of Coquimbo, 30 m. SW of Copiabo, near the N bank of the Salado.
BAAB, a town of Syria, in the pashalik and 25 m. NE of Aleppo.
BAAB (EL), a town of the Sahara, to the S of Fezzan, on the route to Lake Tchad.
BAAD, a small town of Hindostan, in the Doab, in the prov. and 9 m. SW of Agra. BAA
AZUCAR, a small island in the Philippine group, near the E coast of the island of Panay.

AZUELA, a river of Ecuador, in the prov. of Quito, which takes its rise in the Cayamba-Urcu mountains, 40 m. ENE of the town of Quito, and, directing its course to the SE, assumes the name of the Ahuarieu, and unites, after a course of 300 m., with the Napo, at St. Miguel, about 220 m. above the confluence of that river with the Amazon. Its principal tributaries are, on the L, the Guyabeno, and, on the r., the Sardinas.

AZUER, a river of Spain, in the prov. of Murcia, which takes its rise in a ramification of the Sierra Morena, to the E of Villahermosa, and, running to the NW, passes Manzanarcs and Daymiel, and unites with the Guadiana 6 m. below the latter town.
AZUL, a small river of New California, which falls into the N side of the Gilas, 200 m. ENE of the junction of that river with the Colorado.

AZUL-AR, or AR-AZUL, a river of La Plata, which rises in the Sierra Huellucal, and running in a NE direction, discharges itself into the Salado 40 m. above the confluence of that river with the Atlantic, assuming in its course the successive names of the Gualichu and Tapallar.
AZUMAR, or Assumar, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, on the E side of the Sierra Alpedreira, 14 m. SSE of Portalegre, and 4 m. WSW of Arronches.

AZUN, a river of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrenees, which descends from the Pyrenean chain near the SW confines of the dep., and, uniting with the Argellez, falls into the Pau about 1 m. E of Argellez. The valley which it waters,-named from its beauty 'the Eden of the Pyrénées,'-is well cultivated, producing wheat, lint, and millet; and its heights afford excellent pasturage to the numerous herds which graze upon them. It contains 10 villages of considerable extent.

AZURARA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Entre-Douro-e-Minho, and comarca of Porto, on the S side of the embouchure of the Ave, opposite Villa-de-Conde, of which it forms a suburb. It has a handsome church. Pop. 2,500.
AZUTAN, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. of Talavera, on the l. bank of the Tagus.
AZY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cher, and canton of Sancergues. Pop. 1,081.
AZY-LE-VIF, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nievre, cant. of St.-Pierre-le-Moutier. It has blast-furnaces and forges. Pop. 750.
AZZANO, a village of Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, 10 m. SW of Verona. Pop. 400. An engagement between the French and Austrians took place in the neighbourhood of this place in 1799.
BAADEN. See BADEN.

BAAG, an island of Denmark, in the prov. of Laaland, NE of the island of Falster, in N lat 54° 56', and E long. 12° 3'. It is 4 m. in length from E to W, and from 1 to 2 m. in breadth; and contains 2 villages with about 550 inhabitants.
BAAGOE, or BAAG, an island of Denmark, in the Little Belt, in the prov. of Sleswig, in N lat. 55° 18', and E long. 9° 48'.

BAAL'S RIVER, a bay on the W coast of Greenland, in N lat. 64° 14', which runs into the land in a NW direction 70 m. It is filled with islands. Hans Egede, on his first mission of Christian love to Greenland, cast anchor in the mouth of this river on the 3d of July, 1721; and in 1733, the brethren fixed upon a spot for building on, on the SW side of a small peninsula jutting out into the river, 3 m. from the open sea, being the nearest habitable part of the coast, to which they gave the name of New Herrnhut. The native pop. scattered along Baal's river, and the coast and islands at this point, was estimated in 1732 at 200 families, or 2,000 souls. The flood tide here flows from S to N; and the average rise of high tide is 2 fath.

BAALBEC, or Balbec, an ancient city of Syria, celebrated for its magnificent ruins, situated in N lat. 34° 1' 30", E long. 36° 11' [Rennell], 40 m. NW of Damascus, and about the same distance from Tripoli, in the valley of Coele-Syria, between the ranges of Libanus and Antilibanus. This city was sacked and dismantled in 748, and never afterwards recovered its original importance. In 1751, however, it had a pop. of about 5,000, when it was devastated by an earthquake, from the effects of which it seems to have been reduced to a mere village of huts, built of mud or sundried bricks, and inhabited by Amamera Bedouins, and forming a singular contrast in their peculiarly wretched appearance to the magnificent ruins which rise around them. Marshal Marmont, who visited these ruins in 1834, estimated the pop. located or encamped amongst them at 1,200: Mr. Addison, who visited them a year later, at only 200; yet the site is highly favoured by nature in the fertility of the soil, the salubrity of the climate, and the abundance of water. The ancient city stood on an amphitheatre formed by the slope of a hill, near the NE extremity of the plain of Bocat or Bekka, or Bekáa, which extends, in the direction from NE by N to SW by S, towards Tyre, with a width of from 8 m. to 12 m., and opens by a narrow pass, in an easterly direction, towards the vale of the Orontes, and is watered by the Litane or Leontes. Approaching B. from the S, up this plain, the town is first descried at the distance of 4 m. behind a screen of foliage chiefly fine walnut trees; and presently, by a winding path through small patches of garden-ground, the traveller, after passing a little octagonal building, supported by 8 granite columns, which Burckhardt describes as "beautiful," but Lord Lindsay declares to be "a mere jumblement," and probably some sheik's tomb, finds himself before the ruined walls of the town. Portions of them are still standing in various places, but they appear never to have been strong. Dr. Richardson thinks they may have been about 4 m. in circuit; other travellers have estimated them at 5 m. Marmont says the boundary walls were so placed as to include an area in the shape of an isosceles triangle whose apex was the highest point of the enclosure; but Dr. Wilson, a more recent visitor, describes them as "an irregular quadrangle in form," and the subjoined cut represents the outline of the walls as traced by Wood and Dawkins: Leaving the modern village of B. to the E, and descending from the interior of the city, amidst immense quantities of hewn stone and fragments of pillars strewn about in all directions, we arrive, on the edge of the low ground, at an open space (a) which may have formed the portico or grand front to the great temple. This façade, the ascent to which must have been by a flight of steps, is placed on a sort of terrace, 260 ft. in length, and was adorned with 12 columns, which, according to Wood's restoration, were 57 ft. in height: the whole height of the order, with its attic and podium, being 93 ft. From the portico, climbing over a mound of ruins, we enter a hexagonal court (b), 190 ft. long by 266 ft. wide, with various little chambers and niches for idols, the pedestals of which, in many instances, still remain [Wilson], and over which, when Volney visited it, were scattered "not only broken columns, mutilated capitals, fragments of pilasters, entablatures, and cornices, but also some entire shafts, and specimens of the most beautiful ornaments of architecture." At the W end of this court is an opening through which we pass into a second and larger quadrangular court or forum (c), 405 ft. long by 440 ft. wide, (Lord Lindsay says it is a square of 350 ft.,) and perceive at its extremity, facing us, 6 enormous Corinthian columns forming the peristyle of the great temple (d). The court in which we now are seems to have formed a kind of corridor or double colonnade, with chambers on either side of its great aisles. These chambers are open towards the court; but at the further end have pediments, niches, and tabernacles highly ornamented with devices. They are thus described by Mr. Addison, who visited them in 1835: "Passing on to the eastward of the great temple, over fragments of Egyptian granite, of cornices and masses of ruins, you find the enclosed space surrounded by walls richly ornamented with mouldings, circular recesses, and niches for statues, which appear to have formed either the sides of handsome chambers and galleries, or to have been covered porticos, of which the columns and roofs are all gone. The first of these ruined walls to which we came, appeared to have formed the three sides of a saloon, or covered portico, about sixty feet by twenty, adorned with a rich cornice, and highly ornamented recesses and niches for statues, the roofs of which were sculptured in the shape of a large shell; the inclosed space was filled with blocks of stone and broken columns of red Thebaic granite. These walls are composed of single blocks of stone four feet thick, and averaging from ten to twelve feet in length, and five in depth. The inner faces of the stones, cut and sculptured, form the sides of the galleries or porticos. We next came to a circular space, or large recess, about eight yards in diameter, surmounted by a low cupola, open at the top, and ornamented with cornices and carvings in roses and leaves, and six Corinthian pilasters, between which are recesses with shell-shaped roofs; and then to another line of wall and niches, similar to the first, but more ruined and disfigured by the modern Saracenic repairs for the purposes of defence, and so on all round the three sides of this vast inclosure; the plan of the buildings on either side exactly corresponds, semicircular rooms being placed opposite to semicircular rooms, corridors to corridors, &c. Passing through a door at the end, we entered what had been a long and wide room or passage; at each end of it was a square room occupying the projecting bastion; the entrances were between two Corinthian pilasters, of which the capitals at the north end, boldly and beautifully executed, preserve all their original freshness. The floor of one room has given way and tumbled into the vaults beneath. These ruins, though so striking and magnificent, are yet, however, quite second rate when compared with the Athenian ruins, and display in their decorations none of the bold conceptions and the genius which characterize the Athenian architecture. There is a peculiar sameness in the decorations of the friezes, entablatures, and cornices; the ornaments are all alike, and the festoons of grapes and vine-leaves hung on goats' and horses' heads, the pendent bunches of grapes and cupids, however rich in appearance and beautifully chiselled, can never excite such feelings as one small portion of the Panathenaic frieze of the Parthenon, or one of the metopes representing the battle between a Centaur and Lapithæ. There is a genius in these latter, a combination of talent, a soul, fire, and spirit, which are looked for in vain in the Baalbec remains. The great Panathenaic frieze of the Parthenon, which extended all round that temple, with its hundreds of horses and warriors, its spirited grouping and faithful delineation of forms and attitudes, and above it the wars of the Centaurs and Lapithæ, possessed a most exciting interest. The vine-branches and wheat-ears of the temples of Baalbec, although unquestionably very beautiful, yet appear tame in comparison, and cannot certainly be put into competition with these masterpieces of architectural decoration." Lord Lindsay discovered the orb with wings and serpents which figures on every Egyptian temple, in one of the friezes in this court. The great temple (d) appears, from Wood and Dawkins' restoration, to have been a peripteral building, 290 ft. long, by 160 ft. wide, with 10 columns in front, and 19 on the flank: the columns being 7 ft. 10 in. in diam., and 8 ft. 1 in. apart. When Thevot visited the place in 1550, 27 columns of this temple were standing; but when Volney saw it, in 1785, only 6 were erect. These 6 columns still survive, and form the principal object in every view of the ruins: viewed near or far they are "equally worthy of love and admiration,-whether you watch them from a distance, or looking upward from their feet, criticise their details, the chaste ornament of their suffit, their rich frieze, their superb cornice,-and pronounce them faultless." [Lindsay.] Wood and Dawkins estimated the height of these columns, with their pedestals, at 71 ft. 6 in. Volney says their shafts are 58 ft. long, and 21 ft. 8 in. in circumf. Addison gives the same estimate of their height; but states their circumf. at only 15 ft. 8 in. The total height of the pediment must have been 120 ft. It is very doubtful whether this temple was ever built; it seems at least never to have been completed; but at intervals all round the oblong area of the building may be traced almost all the pedestals of the other shattered columns. [Addison and Wilson.] From the portico at the landing-place on the E to the western extremity of the great temple, Lord Lindsay found it to be 336 paces, or above 1,000 ft. The whole is raised on an artificial basement ~ • 13 E C BAALB A Fullation & C. London & Eduburgh 9 0 RUINS THE SET SE Calloott W.

MIL DY. * ex. FEE 100 9 1-73-1- The state of the state of the state of the state of the state of the state of the state of the state of the state of the state of the state of the state of the state of the state of the state of the state of the state of t The same and arched vaults or passages, to some of which access can now be got. [Wilson.] A smaller and more perfect building,—the temple of the Sun (e), -stands directly S of the great temple, on a platform adjoining that of the latter, but considerably lower, the bottom of the basement of the great temple being nearly as high as the top of the basement of the smaller edifice, and the best view of it, commanding its N and W façade, being to be obtained from underneath the six pillars. [Lindsay.] This building has had 8 columns in front, and 15 on the flank, counting the corner columns. It is 225 ft. in length, by 118 ft. in width. Nine pillars on the N side, and 4 on the S, are still standing; of those at the Wend, 4 are broken, 8 are perfect, and 1 has fallen. Of the portico at the E end, 4 columns only, at the SE angle, remain perfect. It consisted of two rows of columns, all fluted, except the two last on either side belonging to the peristyle. These 4 columns are surmounted by a beautiful frieze and cornice; above which a battlemented tower has been reared by the Saracens, who have also erected a huge wall directly in front of the great gate of the temple. "Creeping through a low gateway," says Lord Lindsay, "we found ourselves before this matchless portal. Every ornament that could be introduced into Corinthian architecture is lavished on it, and yet it is perfectly light and graceful." This portal is about 20 ft. in width, and double that in height. It is composed of 9 vast stones, 6 forming the sides, and 3 the top. The keystone, dislodged from its place probably by an earthquake, has slipped partly through, and hangs ominously overhead as one passes under it. The body of an eagle carved on the suffit, and holding in his talons what has been called the caduceus, and ribbons or garlands in his beak, flowing towards a winged genius, has become separated from its wings by this displacement. The gateway is surmounted by a rich cornice and frieze ornamented with leaves, flowers, and fruits; and is surrounded by a richly carved border of vine-leaves, wheat-ears, and pendant bunches of grapes. On either side of the portal is a staircase for ascending to the top of the temple. That on the N can still be ascended. It twists round within a square pillar, and is of beautiful construction. The interior of this temple is also very richly ornamented by 6 fluted Corinthian pillars adhering to the walls on each side, and 3 pilasters supporting a broken entablature, on which is displayed a rich frieze. The columns on the N side still support a richly carved and arched roof of stone, covering the peristyle court, between the columns and inner wall of the temple. This roof is divided into diamond-shaped compartments by rich carvings; and on these lacunari or panels, are sculptured a series of large busts, and mythological designs, each occupying the centre of a large diamond, while smaller busts occupy the angles formed by the interlacing compartments. The columns of this building are each of three pieces, joined very accurately together without cement, and strengthened with immense iron pins worked into a socket in the centre of each stone. The largest of these pins is nearly 12 in. in diam., and as much in length; and they unfortunately have long afforded a sufficient inducement to the barbarous Turks to knock the pillars to pieces to get at the iron. No cement or mortar appears to have been used throughout the building, yet the joints are almost invisible. Dr. Richardson is of opinion that these ruins exhibit traces of two separate people practising different modes of architecture. "The most ancient parts," he says, "are chiefly on the S and W, and are most abundant in the SW corner of the wall." Pococke says that he saw stones in the basement of the great temple, even in the second course, from 29 ft. to 37 ft. in length, and 9 ft. thick; and Marshal Marmont observed stones whose contents measured 5,297 cubic ft.; and Volney, in one of the adjacent quarries, observed a stone not quite detached from the rock, which was 69 ft. 2 in. long, 12 ft. 10 in. wide, and 13 ft. 3 in. deep. The cubic contents of this stone were estimated by Marmont at 15,892 cubic ft. These stones are a compact limestone according to Dr. Richardson, which, he adds, is the common stone of the country. Volney inaccurately says they are of white granite, with large shining veins like gypsum. They are cut with the bevelled edge exactly like the cutting of stones which Dr. Richardson observed in some ancient subterranean structures in Jerusalem; and he is disposed to refer the more ancient part of the B. edifices to the era of Solomon. "The second builders of this enormous pile have built upon the foundations of the former building, and, in order that the appearance of the whole might seem to be of one date, they have cut a new surface upon the old stones. This operation has not been completely finished, and some of the stones remain half-cut, exhibiting part of the old surface, and part of the new, so that the different eras of the building are exemplified in the same stone. We measured two of the stones near the SW corner in the S wall; one of them was 67 ft. long, nearly 14 ft. broad, and 9 ft. thick; the other was 64 ft, long: we could not measure the breadth and thickness. There is a third stone apparently of the same dimensions. These have been partly cut for a new surface, and partly not. They are, perhaps, the most ponderous masses that human hands or human machinery ever moved into a wall; and here they are between 20 and 30 ft. above the foundation. Indeed, I am not acquainted with any building, except the one under consideration, where we can find stones the half of the above dimensions, or even the fourth of it. The northern wall of Jerusalem, which Josephus says was built of stones 30 ft. long, has long since been destroyed, and every stone broken to pieces. In the pyramids of Egypt we saw one or two stones 18 ft. long, and at Koom-Ombos measured one of 23 ft.; but these are but occasional blocks introduced for l BAA purposes of particular security. But a whole wall, or a whole building, of nearly 400 ft. a-side, constructed of stones from 30 to 60 ft. long, is something more than Cyclopean; the labours of Hercules were but a joke to this! However, I am not disposed to think that these immense blocks formed any part of the original wall; they do not harmonize with that which is around them, and the part which is below them is the repaired, and not the original wall. Most probably they were intended for stone columns to serve some ornamental purpose in the interior. The great disproportion of their length to their breadth and thickness,-their being all in the same part of the wall,-and there being none like them in any other part of the building,—seem to authorize the supposition. The place which they occupy in the wall is nearly on a level with the floor of the interior of the building, and hence they were edged into it by the repairer, who knew no better use for them. The northern part of this outside wall is more modern, and, in my opinion, Roman. The stones are much smaller, and it is vaulted below to support the floor within; the southern part seems to be banked up with earth and stone, without arches. So much for the shell of this magnificent structure, which has evidently been a wall of defence to protect the precious structures within." Leaving the temple of the Sun, and crossing a small stream, we approach a beautiful little Corinthian temple (f) circular within and without, 32 ft. in diam. internally, and 63 at its greatest width externally. It is pierced externally with handsome niches, each flanked by two columns, and having the architraves and cornices curving inwardly, so as to give the building the appearance of an octagon, In the ground-plan above inserted g marks the highest ground enclosed within the walls, and the site of what Dr. Pococke calls a very fine Tuscan pillar, but Wood and Dawkins a Doric column. Squire, in 1802, found this column thrown down and destroyed. Lord Lindsay notices another isolated pillar, of Corinthian order, which rises nobly in the middle of the plain nearly due W of B. The supposed sites of the city-gates are marked at i. At what time and by whom these great and mysterious edifices were founded is unknown. Arab tradition refers them to Solomon; and both Richter and Richardson were struck by the resemblance of some portions of the mass of building to the remains of the temple of Solomon, still seen in the foundations of the mosque Es Sakkhara on Mount Moriah. The Sabians or Fire-worshippers, according to an Arab author, cited by Abulfeda, held these edifices in high reverence as the work of their ancestors. The name Heliopolis, given by the Greeks to this city, is a literal translation of the ancient Baalbeth or Baalath, signifying 'the City of Baal' or the Sun, whose worship anciently prevailed amongst the Syrians and Chaldeans; and the early and original name has, in this instance—as in many others in the East—survived the occupation of the country by both the Greeks and the Romans, and prevailed over the name employed by them. The temples themselves, in their upper structure at least, are of Roman origin. John of Malala informs us that Ælius Antoninus Pius built a great temple to Jupiter at Heliopolis. This was probably reared on the site and in part from the remains of a greatly more ancient edifice; but having been built only a short time before the establishment of Christianity in Asia, the temple soon lost its importance as a place of heathen worship, and probably never was actually finished according to the conception of its architects. Theodosius converted some portions into a Christian church.—The city made a vigorous defence against the forces of Caliph Omar; and under the Omniades was a place of considerable importance. It is often mentioned in the chronicles of the Crusaders; but warfare and earthquakes, and the rapacity of successive pashas in later times, appear to have gradually reduced it to a state of comparative insignificance. Baumgarten visited B. in 1507, but appears to have failed to discover the splendour of its architecture under the disguise of what it then was, a Turkish fortress. Belon notices it succinctly. The first detailed account of B. was supplied by Monconys, who visited it in 1647. La Roque spent some time at B. in 1688; and Maundrell in 1696. Pococke's description in 1738 is minute and accurate. Wood's splendid publication, The Ruins of B., left little to be gleaned by future travellers. Volney's account in 1781 is animated and correct. Among the later English travellers who have visited and described B., are Dr. Richardson (Travels along the Mediterranean, &c. Lond., 1822, 2 vols. 8vo.); Mr. C. Addison (Damascus and Palmyra. Lond., 1838, 2 vols. 8vo.); Lord Lindsay (Letters on Egypt, Edom, &c. Lond., 1847, 8vo.); and Dr. Wilson (Lands of the Bible. Edin., 1847, 2 vols. 8vo.).

BAAR, a parish and town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Zug, situated in a fertile plain on the l. bank of the Lorze, 4 m. NE of the confluence of that river with the Zugger-see, and about the same distance from the town of Zug, at an alt. of 1,380 ft. above sea-level. It has a handsome town-house, and an extensive paper-mill; and is a considerable commercial entrepot. Pop. 1,300.
BAARA, a town of Syria, in Haouran, 32 m. NE of Kelb-Haouran, and 43 m. SSE of Damascus.
BAARDWYK, a town of Holland, in N Brabant, 3 m. SW of Huesden, and 15 m. ENE of Breda. $
BAARLAND, a town of Holland, in Zeeland, near the S coast of the island of South Beveland.
BAARLE, a town of Belgium, in E Flanders, W of Ghent. Pop. 1,798.
BAARLE-NASSAU, a town of Holland, in N Brabant, 12 m. SSE of Breda. Pop. 1,550. It was the birth-place of the poet Barlæus.
BAAS-FIORDEN, a gulf of the Icy sea, on the coast of Norway, in the prov. of Finmark.
BAASHEKA, a Yezidee village of Persia, in Diarbekir, 15 m. NE of Mosul. It is surrounded by olive-plantations. The environs present hills of sandstone, limestone, and gypsum.-Rich.
BAAS-TANDAO. See TANDAO-BAAS.
BAATSCH. See BACS.

BABA, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Upper Wallachia, on the l. bank of the Ketiniste, 25 m. above the confluence of that river with the Arjisch, and 30 m. SW of Bukarest. It has some dye-works. Pop. 2,000.—Also a town of Greece, in the prov. of Trikala, on the r. bank of the Selembria, at the SW extremity of the vale of Tempe, 14 m. NNE of Larissa. It was one of the early Turkish settlements of colonists from Iconium; but is now a deserted-looking place. It is surrounded with a ditch and embattled brick walls, flanked with towers. It has some dyeworks. Pop. 2,000.—Also a town of Asia Minor, in the sanjak and to the SW of Biga, near the cape of the same name. It possesses excellent and formerly very extensive manufactories of cutlery, sword-blades, and yataghans; and has a small harbour. The environs are fertile, and afford large quantities of valonia for export. Pop., consisting of Turks and Greeks, 4,000. It is the ancient Lectum; and is sometimes called by the Franks St. Mary's.—Also a mountain of Greece, on the W confines of the prov. of Thessaly, between the Mezzovo and Agrafa, or Pindus ranges. —Also a river which takes its rise in the above mountain, and entering Albania, runs in a SW direction through the sanjak of Janina, and unites with the Aspropotamo 4 m. NNE of Cardiki.—Also a town of Guayaquil, on the N extremity of Lake Sanboramban, 45 m. N of the town of Guayaquil. The cocoanut abounds in the vicinity, and chocolate is extensively manufactured for export.—Also a small river, which rises in the Carguairazo mountains, and uniting with the Caracol, discharges itself into the abovementioned lake.—Also one of the Molucca islands. It is hilly, and some summits attain a considerable alt. The W side is precipitous, and overgrown with forests. On the E side is the town of Aluta.—Also a village on the N coast of the island of Celebes, in the Asiatic archipelago.
BABA-AIOU. See A10U.

BABA-BEG-SHUHRI, or Shuri'-Babic, a town of Persia, in the prov. and 90 m. W of Kirman, on an affluent of Lake Bakhtigan. It is of considerable antiquity. It has four gates, which open into long straight avenues, concentrating in the market-place, the dome of which is the finest in Persia. It was formerly the commercial entrepot of Congoon, Shiraz, Kirman, and Yezd, and is still the residence of the governor of the prov. The approaches on all sides are adorned with fruit-trees, and the gardens are celebrated for their luxuriance.

BABA (CAPE), a bold headland on the coast of Anatolia, the Lectum promontorium of the ancients; in N lat. 39° 30′ 5″, E long. 26° 5′. It is a projecting spur of Mount Ida. A group of islets runs out from it, the Hecatonnessoi of the ancient Greeks.

BABA-DAGH, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Bulgaria, and sanjak of Silistria, at the NW extremity of Lake Rassein, and near the northern base of the Baba-Dagh mountains, 90 m. NE of Silistria, and 20 m. SW of Tultcha. It is strongly BAB fortified as an important post between the hills and morasses. The streets are generally paved. It contains 5 mosques, -one of which, built by Bajazet, is covered with lead, -2 baths, and a college. The water with which the town is supplied, is conveyed by means of an aqueduct from a distance of 3 m., that of the surrounding wells being impure. Commerce is carried on to a considerable extent by the port of Kara-Kirman, on the Black sea. The adjacent locality is marshy. B. owes its existence to the emperor Bajazet, by whom it was erected for the defence of his conquests. Pop., consisting of Turks, Tartars, Greeks, Armenians, and Jews, 10,000.

BABA-DA'GH (Mount Cadmus), an extensive mountain-chain of Asia Minor, in the prov. of Anadolia. It unites with the Enamas mountains on the E, and running in a generally SW direction through the sanjaks of Hamid, Kermian or Kutuva, and Mentesha, terminates at the shore of the Mediterranean opposite the island of Kalimmo. In its range of 270 m. in length, it forms the line of separation between the southern basin of the Meander and that of the Mediterranean.

BABA-GURA, a mountain-ridge, a ramification of the Carpathian chain, which lies to the N of Mount Tatra, and bounds on the N, and penetrates to a considerable extent, the Hungarian comitat of Arva. It consists of calcareous transition rock, and its highest summit is 5,760 ft. in height. It is traversed by a road leading from the vale of the Arva, by the pass of Jordanof, into Galicia.
BABAHOGO. See BABA.
BABA-PULO. See Pulo-Baba.
BABAZECK. See Babinagreda.

BABBA, an island midway between Banda and the N coast of Australia, in S lat. 7° 20'. It is 20 m. in length, and about 7 m. in breadth. The interior abounds with wild cattle, hogs, and goats. The natives seem to be a more barbarous race than those of the adjacent islands.

BABBAGE, a river of Arctic America, which descends from a distant part of the interior, and flows into Phillips bay, in N lat. 69° 19', W long. 138° 10\frac{1}{2}'. At some distance above its mouth it is 2 m. wide.
BABBALIA, or BIBBALIA, a town of Nigritia, in the territory of Begharmi, to the SSE of Lake Tchad, and 180 m. ESE of Kouka.
BABCARY, a parish of Somersetshire, 4 m. E of Somerton. Area 2,450 acres. Pop. 465.
BABEIN, a v. of Persia, 90 m. SE of Kerman.
BABEL, a town of Lower Egypt, in the prov. of Garbieh, on an offset of the Nile, 45 m. NNW of Cairo. It contains considerable ruins, supposed to be those of the ancient Byblos.
BABEL. See BABYLON.
BABEL ISLANDS, a small group in Bass's strait, in the S Pacific, about 4 m. to the E of Great island, in S lat. 40° 0′ 30", E long. 148° 17'.
BABEL (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Issoire. Pop. 1,720.

BABELABUAD. See DERBEND. BAB-EL-MANDEB [i. e. 'the Gate of Tears'], the strait leading from the Indian ocean into the Red sea. The coasts of Asia and Africa, or of Arabia and Abyssinia, here approach within 144 m. of each other. [Haines.] On the Arabian side, a craggy wedge-like shaped cape, Ras Bab-el-Mandeb, rising to an alt. of 865 ft. [Haines], and connected with the continent by a low isthmus, so as to have the appearance from a distance of an island, projects a considerable way from the main land, in N lat. 12° 41' 10", E long. 43° 32′ 14". The opposite approximating point on the Abyssinian or Adel coast, is a volcanic peak called Jebel or Ras Sijan, about 380 ft. in alt. The intermediate space is studded with islands. Within the strait, but nearest to the Arabian shore, is a bare rocky island, called Perím or Meyún, about 41 m. long, by 2 m. broad, rising in the centre to 230 ft. [Moresby], 120 ft. [Welsted]. A few tufts of absinthium and salicornia form the only vegetation of this island. The channel to the E of it is called the Little strait; that to the W of it, the Large strait. The former is from 1 m. at its narrowest part to 4 m. wide, with a depth varying from 9 to 14 fath. on hard sand, and is most frequently used by vessels. The Large strait is from 9 to 10 m. wide. No soundings have been found in mid-channel of this strait with 100 fath. On its W side, 9 m. S of Perim island, and 6 m. E of Jebel Sijan, lies a small volcanic island, called the High Brother, the northernmost of a group of six, called the Brothers, cropping out of a bank on which the soundings vary from 15 to 20 fath. These islets lie nearly due E and W of each other 51 m. Their structure exhibits successive depositary strata, in which lava and coral form a large proportion; and as the coral is in many instances considerably advanced in growth, it seems probable that they have been reared up from the bottom of the ocean by a submarine volcano within a recent period. [Welsted.] Ships may anchor under Ras Bab-el-Mandeb in 61 fath. There is an excellent and well-protected harbour on the SW side of Perim island, whose S point is in N lat. 12° 38°, E long. 43° 28′ 40". The rise of tide at this island is about 6 ft. The currents are generally strong in the strait, and vary in direction according to the prevailing winds. The name-frequently and vulgarly written Bab-el-Mandel-given to this strait is supposed to have been bestowed by early navigators to mark their sense of the perils attendant on its navigation. Edrisi seems to apply the name to the island of Perim; and to consider the passage between it and the Arabian coast as 'the Gate;' for he says, "the passage is so narrow that a man standing on one shore would recognise a person who might be standing on the other."
BAB-EL-MARDIN, a remarkable gap or notch in the chain of Mount Masius, near the city of Mardin.
BABELTHUP, an island in the N Pacific, the largest of the Pelew group, in N lat. 7° 41', E long. 134° 55'. It is 24 m. in length; and on the NE is very mountainous.

BABENHAUSEN, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Upper Danube, on the r. bank of the Gunz, 22 m. above the confluence of that river with the Danube, and 44 m. SW of Augsburg. It is the capital of a seigniory of the same name, and has a fine castle, the residence of the princes of Fugger-Babenhausen. Mining operations are extensively carried on in the vicinity. Pop. 1,666.—Also a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the circle of Starkenburg, on the l. bank of the Gersprenz, 25 m. NE of Darmstadt. It has an ancient castle, formerly the residence of the counts of Hanau-Lichtenberg, now a military house-of-correction, and a college; and conducts a considerable commerce in grain and lint. In the environs are extensive brick-works. Pop., chiefly Lutheran, 1,602.
BABENSK, a town in Russian Lapland, near the S shore of Lake Imandra.

BABERGH, a hundred in the SW of the co. of Suffolk, comprising 32 parishes, and an area of 68,380 acres. Pop. in 1831, 23,678; in 1841, 24,069. BABERT, -corruptedly BAIBURTH, -a district and town of Russian Armenia, formerly in the pashalik of Erzerum. The town lies in a plain, between two mountains, on the Charoch river. Pop. 2,000, chiefly Turks. The principal occupation of the inhabitants is working in metals, and manufacturing carpets which are interwoven with gold thread. BAB
BABI, a small island near the W coast of Ceram, in E long. 128° 3′, S lat. 3° 5′.

BABI (Pulo), an island in the Chinese sea, opposite the entrance to Blair's harbour, in N lat. 2° 43°, E long. 103° 39'.—Also an island, in N lat. 2° 53', E long. 95° 30', 60 m. from the W coast of Sumatra. It is nearly 40 m. in length, and about 5 m. in average breadth.
BABIA, a river of Russia, in Lapland, which falls into the White sea at the cape of the same name; in N lat. 66° 25′, E long. 39° 10′.
BABIACORA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. and near the l. bank of the Sonora, 50 m. SSW of Arispe.
BABICE, a town of Galicia, in the circle and 15 m. WNW of Przemysl, on the l. bank of the San. Pop. 1,100.
BABICSA-OCSAK, a town of Bosnia, to the E of Bosnaserai, on the Krivaja.
BABILAFUENTE, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 13 m. E of the town of Salamanca. Pop. 704. It possesses some mineral springs.
BABIN, a town of Russian Poland, 8 m. SW of Lublin.—Also a town in the Russian gov. of Podolia, 28 m. NE of Bracla.
BABINAGREDA, a town of Austria, on the Military Frontier of Slavonia, SW of Vinkoveze. Pop. 4,200.
BABINGLEY, a parish in Norfolkshire, 2 m. N of Castle-Rising. Area 870 acres. Pop. 54.
BABINGTON, a parish in Somersetshire, 4 m. NW of Frome. Area 600 acres. Pop. 163.
BABINOWITSCHI, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Mohilev, on the r. bank of the Latschossa, an affluent of the Duna, 60 m. N of Mohilew. Pop. 600.
BABINOPOGLIE, a village of Austria, in Dalmatia, on the island of Melida, in the circle of Ragusa. Pop. 594. It is in the centre of a valley about 2 m. in extent, and is surrounded by mountains.
BABISPE, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Chihuahua, near the head of the river of the same name, and 110 m. E by N of Arispe.
BABIVELEKI, a town of Moldavia, on the l. bank of the Sereth, in N lat. 46° 30'.
BABO, or Babu, a town of Guinea, in the Whidah territory, on the river Lagos, in N lat. 6° 54'.
BABOCSA, or Babolcza, a frontier-town of Hungary, in the co. of Schümeg, on the Rinya, 13 m. from Presnitz.

BABOLNA, a village of Hungary, in the comitat of Comorn, celebrated for its stud of horses, and a merino - sheep establishment. - Also a village of Transylvania, in the Hunyad, celebrated for its wines.
BABRAHAM, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 4 m. NW of Linton. Area 2,350 acres. Pop. 217.
BABRIAWAR, a mountainous barren tract on the E side of the SW angle of the peninsula of Guzerat in Hindostan.
BABU, an island in the gulf of Siam, off the W coast of Cambodia, in N lat. 9° 41'.
BABUAN, an island in the Sooloo archipelago, to the E of Tawi-tawi, in N lat. 5° 15', E long 120° 35'.

BABUYA'NES, a cluster of islands in the Pacific, forming part of the Philippines, and lying between the Bashi isles on the N, and the large island of Luzon on the S. They have steep coasts, with deep intervening channels. Babuyan, the most northern of the cluster, is in N lat. 19° 38', E long. 122° 9'. It is about 24 m. in circumf. Four others, viz., Calayan, Camiguen, Dalapiri, and Fuga, are inhabited; the others are mere rocky islets. The inhabitants of the group are about 800 in number; and trade with the Chinese in wax, ebony, cassia, and cocoa-nuts.
